coredata.dc:title: Investigating adsorption characteristics and electronic properties of Clar’s Goblet and beyond

coredata.dc:creator: Sakr, Mahmoud A.S.
Abdelsalam, Hazem
Teleb, Nahed H.
Abd-Elkader, Omar H.
Saroka, Vasil A.
Zhang, Qinfang
coredata.dc:description: Our study explores the structural diversity and stability of Clar’s Goblets (CG) derivatives, nCG-mC, analyzing dihedral angles and bond lengths to reveal various planar and non-planar conformations. We highlight the exceptional stability of 3CG-7C through binding energy assessments. Examining electronic, magnetic, and optical properties, we show how CG unit variations impact energy gaps and molecular orbitals. Functionalizing 3CG-7C enhances stability and electronic properties, suggesting diverse applications. Adsorption studies of chlorinated methane on 3CG-7C-NO demonstrate stable interactions, confirmed by changes in bond lengths and Mulliken charges, underscoring the potential of CG derivatives in various fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Clar’s Goblet
DFT
Chlorinated Methane compounds
Electronic and adsorption properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The molecular optimized structures of CG-based materials are denoted as nCG-mC, where n can be either 2 or 3, and m can be an even number (2 or 4) or an odd number (3 or 7).
HOMO/LUMO, distribution, for spin-up and spin down electrons of CG derivatives. Red and green isosurfaces, respectively, denote positive and negative phases of the orbital wave function.
Spin-up and spin-down DOS for CG derivatives.
Electrostatic potential map (ESP) for CG-based substances.
The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CG-based materials.
Optimized structures and calculated BE for functionalized 3CG-7C (a-h). MESP maps for functionalized 3CG-7C (i-o).
HOMO/LUMO MOs and energy gap (Eg) for functionalized 3CG-7C.

Optimized (a-d), HOMO/LUMO MOs, and energy gap (Eg) (e-l) for 3CG-7C-NO after adsorption of chlorinated methane compounds. Red and blue hues, respectively, denote positive and negative of the orbital wave function.
Some important structural parameters like bond length (Å), dihedral, and bond angles (degrees) for CG and its derivatives.
The calculated excited state (ES), maximum wavelength (λmax), transition energy (TE), electronic transition (ET), oscillator strength (f), and transition coefficient (TC) for CG derivative materials.

The HOMO energy (EH), the LUMO energy (EL), chemical potential (ρ), electronegativity (χ), chemical hardness (η), dipole moment (μ), and electrical conductivity for spin-up of selected CG based materials.

C-Cl, and N-O bond lengths and the Mulkin charge for Cl and N for 3CG-7C-NO-CH3Cl, 3CG-7C-NO-CH2Cl2, 3CG-7C-NO-CHCl3, and 3CG-7C-NO-CCl4 before and after the adsorption of chlorinated methane compounds. Also calculated the adsorption energy (Eads.).

head.ce:title: Investigating adsorption characteristics and electronic properties of Clar’s Goblet and beyond

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Our study explores the structural diversity and stability of Clar’s Goblets (CG) derivatives, nCG-mC, analyzing dihedral angles and bond lengths to reveal various planar and non-planar conformations. We highlight the exceptional stability of 3CG-7C through binding energy assessments. Examining electronic, magnetic, and optical properties, we show how CG unit variations impact energy gaps and molecular orbitals. Functionalizing 3CG-7C enhances stability and electronic properties, suggesting diverse applications. Adsorption studies of chlorinated methane on 3CG-7C-NO demonstrate stable interactions, confirmed by changes in bond lengths and Mulliken charges, underscoring the potential of CG derivatives in various fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional (2D) materials, exemplified by graphene, transition metal dichalcogenides, and black phosphorus, have captivated significant attention due to their unique attributes and wide-ranging potential applications [1]. Graphene, a monolayer of carbon atoms configured in a honeycomb lattice, exhibits remarkable mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties, positioning it as an auspicious material across various domains, including electronics, energy storage, and sensors [2]. Transition metal dichalcogenides like molybdenum disulfide and tungsten diselenide, boasting direct bandgaps and robust light-matter interactions, emerge as promising contenders for electronic and optoelectronic applications [3]. Black phosphorus, with its anisotropic properties, demonstrates promise in applications like transistors, photodetectors, and energy storage devices [4,5]. The distinct characteristics of 2D materials render them highly promising across diverse applications, propelling ongoing research in this dynamic field.

The exploration of two-dimensional quantum dots, essentially 2D nanocrystals, has gained momentum owing to their size-dependent properties and potential applications in optoelectronics and photonics [6,7]. These quantum dots, confined in two dimensions, display discrete energy levels and tunable optical properties, setting them apart from their three-dimensional counterparts [8,9]. The larger surface-to-volume ratio of 2D quantum dots enhances surface chemistry, affording greater control over optical and electronic properties [10,11]. Integration with other 2D materials, such as graphene and transition metal dichalcogenides, further positions 2D quantum dots as integral components for hybrid nanodevices [12–14]. Ongoing research in this domain holds great promise for applications in solar cells, light-emitting diodes, and quantum computing.

Two-dimensional materials, such as graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots have emerged as pivotal players in wastewater treatment, attributed to their high surface area, tunable pore size, and superior adsorption capabilities [15–17]. Graphene oxide membranes have proven effective in efficiently removing heavy metal ions, organic dyes, and pharmaceuticals from wastewater. Covalent and metal–organic frameworks, characterized by high porosity and surface area, offer selective adsorption of contaminants, synthesized with tailored chemical properties and pore sizes for precise wastewater treatment [18,19]. These materials also contribute significantly to membrane technology for wastewater treatment, with potential scalability in production and application [20]. Overall, 2D porous materials demonstrate great promise for efficient and selective removal of contaminants from wastewater, with ongoing and robust research in this burgeoning field [21].

Clar’s Goblet (CG), a nanographene quantum dot with fused aromatic rings and two unpaired electrons, has garnered attention for its potential applications in electronics, energy storage, and catalysis [22–30]. Recent synthetic advancements have enabled the characterization of CG, solidifying its position as a candidate for designing advanced materials. The hexagonal shape and dual reactive sites of CG have shown promise in improving charge transport properties in organic solar cells and exploring new avenues in the development of organic magnets. The confinement of two unpaired electrons within the CG molecule further underscores its potential for diverse applications, marking a significant milestone in ongoing research within this dynamic field [22–29]. This manuscript explores the structural intricacies and electronic characteristics of Clar's Goblet derivatives, specifically nCG-mC compounds. Our investigation reveals fascinating features, including planarity variations and distinct dihedral angles, elucidating the unique structural aspects of these materials. Calculated binding energies underscore the exceptional stability of CG-based compounds, surpassing previously studied frameworks. Electronic analyses offer insights into the reactivity and electronic structure of these derivatives, with a focus on the impact of functional groups. Furthermore, our study demonstrates the promising adsorption capabilities of 3CG-7C-NO, suggesting its potential applications in environmental remediation.

The electronic, electrostatic potential (ESP), magnetic, and optical properties of CG-based materials are being investigated using Gaussian 16 with density functional theory (DFT) simulations. The hybrid B3LYP functional [31,32] is employed due to its known adequacy in representing electronic and optical properties of C-based structures [33–36]. The basis set chosen is 6-31G for computational efficiency while maintaining acceptable accuracy [37–39]. Time-dependent DFT calculations [40,41] are utilized for the first twenty excited states to analyze optical properties. Additionally, the electronic properties of 3CG-7C-NO post-adsorption of chlorinated methane compounds are studied using the 6-31G/B3LYP level of theory.

In this study, the optimized molecular structures (MSs) of Clar's Goblet (CG) derivatives, labeled as nCG-mC with varying numbers of CG units (n) and linked carbon atoms between CG units (m), were analyzed. The results are presented in Fig. 1 (a-h) and summarized in Table 1 . The structural analysis revealed intriguing insights into CG-based compounds. Specifically, structures in Fig. 1 (a-d) were found to be completely planar, while 3CG-3C and 3CG-7C (Fig. 1 (g and h)) exhibited non-planar conformations due to steric hindrance, with the central CG units rotating to a perpendicular orientation relative to the left and right CG units.

Furthermore, 2CG-3C and 2CG-7C (Fig. 1 (e-d)) showed smaller rotations of each CG unit compared to 2CG-2C, 3CG-2C, 3CG-3C, and 2CG-4C (Fig. 1 (a-d)), attributed to the odd number of carbon atoms in the bridge between CG units in the latter two compounds. The planarity of all CG units was assessed based on dihedral angles, which fell within a narrow range across all CG-based materials studied. The energy gain (ΔE) between the planar (3CG-2C) and nonplanar (3CG-3C) structures is 380.696814 Hartree. This positive energy gain indicates that the planar structure is more stable (lower energy) than the nonplanar structure. The analysis of the energy gain would involve understanding why the planar structure exhibits lower energy compared to the nonplanar structure. Factors such as bond angles, steric hindrance, conjugation effects, and strain energy may contribute to this energy difference.

Additionally, bond angles within the structures indicated sp2 hybridization of the hexagonal groups, and variations in C-C bond lengths were observed, reflecting both single and double bond properties. These findings contribute valuable insights into the structural characteristics, planarity, dihedral angles, and bond lengths of CG and its derivatives, enhancing our understanding of their electronic and chemical properties.
Top of Form.

In our investigation focusing on the stability assessment of CG derivatives, we calculated their binding energies (BE). The BE was determined using the formula: BE = (NCEC + NHEH − Et)/Nt, where NC, NH, and Nt represent the numbers of carbon, hydrogen, and total atoms in the compound, respectively. EC, EH, and Et correspond to the total energies of the carbon atoms, hydrogen atoms, and the final compound, respectively. Our analysis of the computed binding energies aimed to confirm the stability and characteristics of CG derivatives. The calculated positive binding energies ranged between 10.856 and 11.070 eV, indicating stable formation of CG-associated compounds.

Specifically, the binding energies for 2CG-2C, 3CG-2C, 2CG-4C, 3CG-4C, 2CG-3C, 2CG-7C, 3CG-3C, and 3CG-7C were 10.856, 10.924, 10.903, 10.985, 10.877, 10.977, 10.952, and 11.070 eV, respectively. These values demonstrate the impact of both the number of CG units and the number of carbon atoms in the bridge between CG units on the stability of CG-based molecular structures. Notably, 3CG-7C exhibited the highest stability among the studied compounds based on the calculated BE values.

Our investigation into binding energies provides strong evidence for the stable formation of CG derivatives, with 3CG-7C standing out as the most stable configuration. Compared to previous studies on biphenyl boroxine covalent organic framework (COF) and its derivatives, where reported BE values ranged from 6 to 10 eV [19], our research underscores the exceptional stability of CG-based materials. The calculated BE values for CG derivatives, ranging from 10.856 to 11.070 eV, clearly indicate their superior stability compared to biphenyl boroxine COF [19]. The stability of biphenyl boroxine covalent organic frameworks (COFs) modified with CN, COOH, and NO2 groups compared to pristine COFs using density functional theory. The interaction with heavy metal pollutants (Hg+2, Pb+2, Cd+2), revealing enhanced intermolecular bonding, reduced band gaps, increased reactivity, and the formation of strong complexes detectable by red shifts in optical absorption spectra [19].This highlights the promising potential of CG derivatives as candidates for stable and robust materials across various applications.

Accurately characterizing the electronic properties of the studied structure is a crucial initial step, requiring an investigation into its magnetic behavior. The ground state energy calculations for various magnetic configurations—including nonmagnetic (closed shell), antiferromagnetic (open shell), and ferromagnetic states with different spin multiplicities—are detailed in Table S1. Comparing these configurations allows us to identify the most energetically favorable state for each system. For most structures, the ground state energy corresponds predominantly to the antiferromagnetic state. However, an exception is observed in the 2CG-7C configuration, illustrated in Fig. 2 d, where the spin configuration reveals a ferromagnetic triplet state due to the presence of unpaired electrons from the third carbon atom.

Figure S2 depicts the spin density distributions of various CG derivatives. Spin density refers to the probability of finding an unpaired electron at a specific location within a molecule [42]. It helps visualize the distribution of electrons and understand magnetic properties [42]. Blue color denotes spin-up electrons, and green denotes spin-down electrons. The figure shows seven panels (a) through (g), each containing a different CG derivative molecule. The net spin (S) of each molecule is indicated next to the panel. Briefly, panels (a) through (f) depict various CG derivatives with a net spin of 0. The spin density is concentrated around specific carbon atoms (a) throughout the molecule, (b) centrally, or (c-e) attached to the CG group. Panel (f) shows a CG derivative with a net spin of 0, where the spin density is concentrated around a single carbon atom attached to the CG group. Panel (g) depicts another derivative, but the net spin is 1 indicated. The spin density is again concentrated around the carbon atoms attached to the CG group.

In the antiferromagnetic cases, both spin-up and spin-down molecular orbitals exhibit nearly identical energy gaps and distributions of the highest occupied/lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (HOMO/LUMO). Our analysis delves into the electronic characteristics of these orbitals, as illustrated in Fig. 2 and Supplementary Figure S1 (see supplementary material). Conversely, in the 2CG-7C, there are discernible differences in the HOMO and LUMO distributions between spin-down and spin-up orbitals. Understanding the energy levels of the HOMO and LUMO for both spin-up and spin-down states is pivotal in grasping the electronic traits of the systems under study. The HOMO energy (EHOMO) signifies the capacity for electron donation, whereas the LUMO energy (ELUMO) indicates the ability to withdraw electrons [43]. In Fig. 2 and Supplementary Figure S1, we present visual representations of the spin-up and spin-down HOMO and LUMO states for CG derivatives. These molecular orbitals showcase localized patterns in specific regions across the molecular structures of CG derivatives.

Fig. 3 illustrates the calculated energy levels of spin-up and spin-down HOMO and LUMO states in the gaseous state for CG derivatives. The energy gap (Eg), defined as the difference between EHOMO and ELUMO [43], is computed for all the studied compounds in Fig. 3. The Eg values range from 1.496 eV to 1.764 eV, with the order of Eg values for spin-up states following 3CG-4C < 2CG-4C < 3CG-2C < 3CG-7C < 2CG-2C < 2CG-7C < 2CG-3C = 3CG-3C. Increasing the number of CG units correlates with a decrease in the Eg value, indicating that structural modifications involving the number of CG units significantly impact the spin-up energy gap in these systems. Fig. 3 shows that the Eg values for spin-up and spin-down states are the same for all studied compounds, except for 2CG-7C, where the Eg of spin-up (1.658 eV) is lower than that of spin-down (2.089 eV). Fig. 3 also depicts the spin-up and spin-down electronic density of states (DOS) for CG derivatives.Fig. 4 .

The energy differences between magnetic (antiferromagnetic or ferromagnetic) and nonmagnetic states (ΔEm) are crucial for understanding the stability and electronic properties of compounds. It is calculated from the Ising model of the honeycomb lattice that can be written as: KBTC/|J| = 0.3797, where KB is Boltzmann constant, TC is the temperature, and J is the magnetic coupling constant equals ΔEm/2 [14]. For instance, ΔEm for 3CG-7G equals −0.863 eV, namely the difference between the ground state energy of the open-shell state and the nonmagnetic state. This means that the antiferromagnetic state in 3CG-7G is stable and can survive up to a temperature of 1901 K. Here are the ΔEm values for the considered structures 2CG-2C: ΔE ≈ −0.837 eV, 3CG-2C: ΔE ≈ −0.851 eV, 2CG-4C: ΔE ≈ −0.854 eV, 3CG-4C: ΔE ≈ −0.901 eV, 2CG-3C: ΔE ≈ −0.829 eV, 3CG-3C: ΔE ≈ −0.852 eV, 3CG-7C: ΔE ≈ −0.863 eV.

The molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analysis is a critical aspect of understanding the reactivity and chemical behavior of Clar's Goblet (CG) derivatives. In this section, we delve deeper into the structural dependence of MEP, considering the arrangement of CG units. The MEP studies provide valuable insights into electrophilic and nucleophilic sites within CG derivatives, aiding in predicting and understanding their chemical behavior and interactions across applications [44]. The MEP analysis revealed distinct patterns, with negative regions concentrated at the extremities, particularly within benzene rings, suggesting nucleophilic sites capable of donating electrons during reactions. Positive regions around terminal hydrogen atoms signify electrophilic sites ready to accept electrons, while neutral regions indicate relative electron neutrality. The arrangement of CG units within derivatives plays a crucial role in determining the distribution of MEP. For example, in planar configurations where CG units are aligned, the MEP tends to be more evenly distributed across the molecule. However, in non-planar configurations with steric hindrance or conformational changes, the MEP distribution may vary significantly.

In this section, we conducted an extensive examination of the UV–Vis absorption spectra for a range of materials based on CG. These materials include 2CG-2C, 3CG-2C, 2CG-4C, 3CG-4C, 2CG-3C, 2CG-7C, 3CG-3C, and 3CG-7C. The resulting spectra are depicted in Fig. 5 , and the pertinent parameters are summarized in Table 2 . Our primary objective was to scrutinize the influence of increasing the number of CG units and the number of carbon atoms within the bridge connecting these CG units on the UV–Vis absorption spectra. These absorption spectra are linked to the electronic properties of the materials and result from electronic transitions between occupied and unoccupied molecular orbitals (MOs).

The study revealed a pronounced impact of increasing the number of CG units and carbon atoms within the bridge on the absorption spectra of CG-based compounds, exemplified by a redshift as shown in Fig. 5 and Table 2. For instance, materials like 3CG-2C, 2CG-4C, and 3CG-4C exhibited a redshift in their absorption spectra compared to 2CG-2C. The λmax values for these transitions were 822.75 nm, 791.86 nm, and 1044.3 nm, corresponding to H → L + 3, H-1 → L + 2, and H-1 → L + 2 transitions, respectively. In contrast, the absorption spectrum of 2CG-2C featured a λmax at 734.60 nm, corresponding to the H-1 → L + 1 transition. In summary, our thorough investigation of the UV–Vis absorption spectra of diverse CG-based materials highlights the significant influence of increasing the number of CG units and carbon atoms within the bridge on the optical properties. The observed redshifts and specific transitions provide valuable insights into the electronic structure and behavior of CG-based materials.

The quantum stability chemical (QSC) parameters, including dipole moment (μ), chemical potential (ρ), electronegativity (χ), and chemical hardness (η), were meticulously determined for spin-up electrons by leveraging the HOMO energy (EH) and the LUMO energy (EL). These key parameters were calculated employing the following equations [45–47]:
ρ =
E H
+
E L
2
, χ
= -
E H
+
E L
2
,
a n d
η =
E L
-
E H
2
. The values of critical quantum-chemical parameters for the investigated structures are presented in Table 3 . Notably, among these structures, 3CG-2C stands out with the highest magnitude of dipole moment (μ), indicating inherent asymmetry in its spin-up electronic charge distribution. This suggests that 3CG-2C experiences a heightened level of active intramolecular charge transfer compared to other structures. Conversely, 3CG-4C exhibits the most negative chemical potential (ρ), indicating a greater propensity for spin-up electrons to escape. The elevated electronegativity values for 3CG-4C underscore its robust electron-attracting characteristics. Moving forward, chemical hardness (η), a measure of resistance to charge transfer, peaks in the 3CG-7C compound, showcasing its superior resilience against charge transfer compared to other structures. In contrast, 3CG-4C reveals the lowest hardness value, suggesting a diminished resistance to charge transfer.Table 4 .

The study also delves into the electrical conductivity of CG derivatives for spin-up electrons, computed using the equation σ ∝ exp(−Eg/2KT) [48], where σ represents electric conductivity, K is Boltzmann's constant, T symbolizes thermodynamic temperature, and Eg denotes the band gap value of the configurations. It's essential to note that a smaller band gap (Eg) value at a given temperature corresponds to higher electrical conductivity. The analysis reveals that the band gaps for 3CG-4C and 2CG-4C show minimal variation, indicating superior electrical conductivity compared to other compounds. In contrast, 2CG-3C demonstrates the lowest conductivity among the investigated materials. The number of carbon atoms in the bridge between CG units and the quantity of SZ units in the structures exert a discernible influence on electrical conductivity, as outlined in Table 3. In summary, the calculation of quantum-chemical parameters and electrical conductivity offers valuable insights into the electronic characteristics and stability of CG derivatives. These findings highlight the attractive electronic properties of CG-based materials, positioning them as promising candidates for various applications in electronic and optoelectronic devices.

In our investigation focusing on the variation in Binding Energy (BE) within CG-based materials, we particularly concentrated on 3CG-7C, which exhibits a notably high BE of 11.070 eV. Our objective was to explore the impact of different functional groups, specifically CN, CO, COOH, NH2, NO, OH, and SO2H, on the stability of 3CG-7C. The BE values were determined using a formula: BE = (NCEC + NHEH + NOEO + NNEN + NSES − Et) / Nt. Here, NC, NH, NO, NN, NS, and Nt represent the numbers of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, and the total atoms in the compound, respectively. EC, EH, EO, EN, ES, and Et correspond to the total energies of the carbon atoms, hydrogen atoms, oxygen atoms, nitrogen atoms, sulfur atoms, and the final compound, respectively. In Fig. 6 (a-h), we present the optimized structures and calculated BE for the functionalized derivatives of 3CG-7C, namely 3CG-7C-CN, 3CG-7C-CO, 3CG-7C-COOH, 3CG-7C-NH2, 3CG-7C-NO, 3CG-7C-OH, and 3CG-7C-SO2H. Our analysis reveals that all of these functional groups, except for NH2, contribute to an increase in the stability of 3CG-7C.

To gain further insights into the electronic properties of these functionalized compounds, we provide the Electrostatic Potential Maps (ESP) in Fig. 6 (i-o). These maps depict negatively charged areas in red, positively charged regions in blue, and neutral zones in green. A significant observation is the concentration of negative regions around the functional groups, particularly NO, CO, COOH, NO, NH2, OH, and SO2H, suggesting their nucleophilic nature. Comparing the MEPs of 3CG-7C with those of the functionalized 3CG-7C, we observe a shift of negative regions towards the functional groups, indicating their propensity to donate electrons during reactions. Positive regions around terminal hydrogen atoms represent electrophilic sites, ready to accept electrons. This analysis provides valuable insights into the impact of functional groups on the stability and electronic properties of 3CG-7C and its derivatives, offering understanding into their reactivity and chemical behavior potential.

In Fig. 7 , graphical representations of electron HOMO and LUMO states for functionalized 3CG-7C are presented. Across the molecular structures of 3CG-7C derivatives, the HOMO and LUMO molecular orbitals exhibit localized patterns in specific regions. The functional groups CN, COOH, NH2, NO, and OH show localized HOMO due to the presence of lone pair electrons. The energy gap (Eg), defined as the difference between EHOMO and ELUMO, is computed for all studied compounds in Fig. 7. The order of Eg values for functionalized 3CG-7C molecular states is as follows: 3CG-7C-NO (0.054 eV) < 3CG-7C-CN = 3CG-7C-COOH = 3CG-7C-NH2 = 3CG-7C-OH = 3CG-7C-SO2H (0.055 eV) < 3CG-7C-CO (1.597 eV) < 3CG-7C (1.610 eV). Therefore, all functional groups decrease the Eg values compared to 3CG-7C.

Fig. 8 (a-d) visually presents the optimized molecular structures for 3CG-7C-NO following the adsorption of chlorinated methane compounds, namely methyl chloride (CH3Cl), methylene chloride (CH2Cl2), chloroform (CHCl3), and carbon tetrachloride (CCl4). Our investigation of the adsorption behavior of chlorinated methane compounds on 3CG-7C-NO, the observed changes in bond lengths, and Mulkin charges provide compelling evidence for successful adsorption. The consistent increase in C-Cl and N-O bond lengths post-adsorption suggests the formation of bonds between the chlorinated methane compounds and 3CG-7C-NO, indicative of a stable interaction. Concurrent shifts in Mulkin charges for chlorine and nitrogen further support the notion of electronic redistribution during the adsorption process. The negative adsorption energies affirm the exothermic nature of adsorption, with varying magnitudes indicating the strength of interactions. Specifically, CH2Cl2 exhibits the strongest interaction, while CH3Cl, CHCl3, and CCl4 demonstrate moderate interactions. These findings collectively underscore the successful adsorption of chlorinated methane compounds on 3CG-7C-NO, with the type and strength of interactions varying based on the specific compound involved. Adsorption energy serves as a crucial metric in discerning the nature of adsorption processes, commonly employed to differentiate between chemical and physical adsorption [49]. According to established criteria [50], when the adsorption energy falls within the range of −0.311 to −0.104 eV, the interaction is characterized as physical adsorption. Conversely, if the adsorption energy is situated between −9.949 and −0.518 eV [50], it signifies the occurrence of chemical adsorption. In our investigation, the calculated adsorption energies for chlorinated methane compounds on 3CG-7C-NO, ranging from −0.314 to −0.102, align with these criteria. The substantial negative value for CH2Cl2 (−0.314) suggests a stronger chemical interaction, while the moderately negative values for CH3Cl, CHCl3, and CCl4 (−0.217 to −0.102) imply physical adsorption, consistent with the observed trends in bond lengths and Mulkin charges.

Fig. 8 (e-l) provides a visual representation of the HOMO/LUMO and the energy gap for 3CG-7C-NO post-adsorption of chlorinated methane compounds. The HOMO and LUMO are localized on the right and left terminal benzene groups, as well as the chlorinated methane groups, with no discernible HOMO and LUMO. A comparison of the energy gap (Eg) values of 3CG-7C-NO before and after the adsorption of chlorinated methane compounds reveals no significant change, indicating stability in the electronic structure after the adsorption process. The lack of alteration in Eg values underscores the robustness of the electronic configuration of 3CG-7C-NO following the adsorption of chlorinated methane compounds.

In conclusion, our in-depth exploration of Clar's Goblet (CG) derivatives has provided valuable insights into their structural, electronic, and adsorption properties. The analysis of optimized molecular structures revealed unique planarity variations and distinctive dihedral angles, highlighting the structural diversity within this molecular family. The calculated binding energies demonstrated the exceptional stability of CG-based materials, surpassing previously studied frameworks, suggesting their potential as robust candidates for various applications. Electronic analyses shed light on the intriguing electronic properties, emphasizing the impact of both the number of CG units and carbon atoms in the bridge between them. Additionally, the successful adsorption of chlorinated methane compounds by 3CG-7C-NO showcases its potential as an effective adsorbent, with implications for environmental remediation. Overall, this comprehensive study contributes to a deeper understanding of CG derivatives, emphasizing their promising prospects across diverse fields.

This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361, 62174016), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211361), and College Natural Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (20KJA430004). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R468), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. V.A.S. acknowledges support by HORIZON EUROPE MSCA-2021-PF-01 (project no. 101065500, TeraExc).
Mahmoud A.S. Sakr: . Hazem Abdelsalam: Writing – review & editing. Nahed H. Teleb: . Omar H. Abd-Elkader: Methodology. Vasil A. Saroka: Validation. Qinfang Zhang: Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361, 62174016), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211361), and College Natural Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (20KJA430004). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R468), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. V.A.S. acknowledges support by HORIZON EUROPE MSCA-2021-PF-01 (project no. 101065500, TeraExc).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2024.141428.

ce:section.ce:para:
